ops.title: Shell expansion safety in command rewrites
ops.body:
## The trap

`shlex.quote()` wraps a token in **single quotes**, and single quotes suppress `~` and `$VAR` expansion even though the rewritten command runs through a real shell. A rewrite that lexically extracts a raw path token (`sed -n '5,10p' ~/x.md` → `ccx code read '~/x.md' --section 5-10`) permanently strips the expansion the original command would have received — the backend then fails with a baffling `not found: /repo/~/x.md`. This produced a 159-instance corpus error family (F7 bucket a, diagnosed 2026-07-13).

Command substitution is worse: the parser drops a standalone `$(…)`/backtick operand entirely, so a rewrite can silently *widen* the command (`grep foo $(printf /tmp/t)` → repo-wide `ccx code grep foo`).

## The rule (per-occurrence, lane-dependent)

Detect with `carries_expansion(token)` (leading `~` or `$` opening a variable name — a mid-word `~` or regex `$`-anchor is not expansion) and `has_command_substitution(raw)`:

- **Bounded-read rewrites** (`sed_rewrites`, `cat_rewrites`, `headtail_rewrites`): the condition declines to match → **Allow**. The original command is a harmless bounded read; letting the real shell run it restores expansion.
- **Flood guards** (`grep_guards`, `rg_guards`): expansion forfeits **only the rewrite lane**, per occurrence — a Block never splices paths, so flood-blocking still fires for that occurrence and for every sibling occurrence independently. Never use a line-level skip: one expandable operand must not launder a sibling `grep bar .`.
- **Generated-command prefixes**: if reconstructing an env/wrapper prefix (`env FOO='two words' ccx …`) would require re-quoting, decline the rewrite and let the Block lane fire — a corrupted rewrite is worse than a block.

`find_rewrites`/`ls_rewrites` embed globs in double quotes: only `~` needs the decline there (`$` expands inside double quotes).

## Known-conservative edges (deliberate, don't "fix")

- Single-quoted literals (`cat '$d/main.go'`) decline even though the shell would treat them literally — the parser exposes no per-token quoting, and an unguarded Allow is safe.
- `env -u X` / `sudo -u user` / `builtin command` wrapper shapes bypass source-executable resolution (fail open to Allow) — option-value-aware unwrapping is an upstream captain-hook gap.
ops.when: before adding a new command rewrite that splices a parsed path or operand into a generated command (anything using shlex.quote)
